author: @david-breitenfeld
text: Two important factors to keep in mind about the "type" of rocket stove: 1) "open" air with full air entering the burn chamber and exiting by short exhaust tube; and 2) "Restricted" air with the air being restricted due to 1) wood in feeder/hopper reduces the air amount and flow; 2) exhaust once exiting the vertical burn riser it is in an enclosed air space and then out into a long chimney.  All this changes the flame motion and burn efficiency dynamics. Roaring flame and air causes a different burn than a "slower" flame. I see this in my home rocket stove as I don't get that nice fast and furious flame vortex, but a milder vortex. This will have to "recreate" different changes for a "restricted air" rocket stove than "open air". Can't really duplicate the design to be used on both. The idea is to find what design and air/flame/flow creates a hot burn, clean exhaust and high extraction of heat before leaving the unit. Remember roaring fire burns out your steel so protect it.

author: @jonsworkshop
text: Hi Robert, been thinking about these for a long time. Best bang for buck has to be shrouded, with a coil heat exchanger inside the shroud for hot water, and a fan in the flue to drive a pump, all built into a mass storage construction. Hot water, space heat, thermal storage and electricity all from very little sold fuel. Has to be one of the most efficient energy transformation devices in this configuration. Please check out Loz Harrop on YouTube, he has built many prototypes, perfecting the design at each iteration, culminating in one of the most efficient rocket stoves I have seen based on his input fuel amounts v/s his output temperature data. Keep playing, these things are awesome and have so many possibilities. Cheers, Jon

author: @justinw1765
text:
Re: Rocket stoves more specifically Rob, what do you think about using vacuum insulated, large glass jars (very easy to do) as the "core" of a rocket stove, but protected by insulated clay (something like cob, but insulated better i.e. instead of using sand, use fumed silica)?

    The idea is to super contain the heat where you want it for an extremely efficient burn, and let the heat out where you want/need it. The trick is to extra seal up the lids to maintain a long term vacuum. The other trick is, as mentioned above, is to protect the glass from heating and cooling too fast, with some insulation and mass (soda glass doesn't do well with expansion to contraction phases). 

   I have a super simple, inexpensive hand/manual pump system that pulls a surprising 28" Hg (Pump N Seal).  Since the jars I would be using are 32 oz, these are a few inches wide. That is a simply ridiculous amount of insulation in the center of the jar. We're talking something like an R150 equivalent (plus whatever the insulated clay adds).  (Granted, the weak park of the system/idea is that you have to use multiple jars, connected to each other, and the space between the jars will have very low insulation comparatively).

replies.author: @daphneraven6745
replies.text:
Justin W:  Have you already tried this? Glass jars usually have a fairly low melting point. Think back to all the times in the chemistry and physics lab when you made your own vessels from existing tempered glass vessels. So it might be important to consider the temperature to which it needs to stand up for your stove.  
     When you talk about using vacuum insulated glass jars, are you talking about something with double walls which take a lid? 

If you have a video, a link would sure be appreciated. :-)

author: @jasonburguess
text: What if you used a standard wood stove catalyst on the rocket stove and then capture the converted gasses to be used to run an engine like a  wood gasifier? Modern wood stoves have a catalyst inside, made to react the volatile gasses and produce more heat, but the end products should still be capable of running a gasoline engine and may be more efficient than a traditional imbert gasifier. Worth an experiment or two. And it might produce significantly less tar and water vapor than the traditional designs developed in the 1930s and 1940s. I would love to see you experiment with this idea. Love the videos, keep em coming!

author: @Aldizerboer
text:
Today I've combined the rocket stove idea from previous video (drilling holes in the bodem of the 'exhaust' part) with the catalytic converter heater. 

I've took a catalytic converter, cut of one side and then put heat resistant wool (from a cat) in the bodem.

Then I've drilled some holes in the top part with a small part of exhaust still on it (so it works as a chimney)

Then I've poured some ethanol inside it and lit it.

Works like a charm till now and looking from the top it almost looks like a gas stove burning wise!

It's been on for 15 minutes now and my shack 4x5m (although quite isolated) the temp has raised 5 degrees celcius. 

And the exhaust gasses are almost not there because the flames are most of the time blue. (yeah I have a window partially opened) 

If you want some pictures please contact me :) it's quite a bodge how I've made it but it works.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Sounds like a borosilicate glass rocket stove would work best for you in that case.  They make high temp adhesives (usually a kind of modified waterglass essentially) that would work especially well with glass (waterglass sticks very well to glass). The downside to b. glass is that it is fairly expensive and somewhat fragile and heavy (but less so than soda glass).  But it all doesn't have to be made out of it.  Just the riser/chimney part of it could be (that would give you plenty of light). 

   When we say campfire, are we talking car camping or backpacking more so?

author: @ur_quainmaster7901
text:
My use case would be a small rocket stove water heater. Heat an insulated water tank via thermosiphon, run hot water indoors into one of those ebay peltier modules with a water block on one side and heatsink/fans on the other. Run the peltiers at low voltage and cool the water/heat the room. This way I don't have to babysit a fire all night... What is stopping me is I live in an area that is only cold for 40-60 days a year. It would take years for ROI to make sense even if I made it from mostly scraps and say, aircrete for insulating the tank. 

The evolved idea would be the same small rocket stove heating a gasifier chamber, water cooling the condenser tanks and using same water for indoor heating mentioned above. Nighthawkinlight had a fine video series on collecting the gas and I've seen it taken further and compressed... though storing hydrogen in standard propane tanks scares me. Carbon fiber scuba tanks. Might require an additional cooling stage if the water tank gets too hot to condense the woodgas.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
It is somewhat easy and not terribly expensive to make vacuum insulated panels at home.  Hence, if you used these, you could use a cheaper material for the tank itself and just line it with these, and achieve super insulation.  

   Btw, the best heat sink for peltier would be a flat panel equivalent of the heat pipes they use to cool computer systems.  The latter are copper tubes that have some wicking material inside and a little bit of distilled water, then a medium vacuum is pulled on it and it is sealed up.  The combination of the copper's conductivity with the phase change cycle of the water inside (from liquid to gas and back and forth), essentially makes it like a super thermal conductor.

     So do this with flat plates of copper instead.  And on top of that, put a microfiber cloth, and just spray with water occasionally (if you're not around, put some kind of container on top, pour water into it along with the wicking and surface area increasing material of your choice).  Voila, you now have a way to really keep it cooler.  Probably the best way to connect the peltier to the copper super cooling plate, would be through a thin layer of indium. 

   Oh, and for the rocket stove.  The core of the rocket stove could be vacuum insulated, large glass bottles that are protected by extra insulated cob of some kind (by extra insulated, instead of using sand to strengthen the cob, use a combo of fumed silica and diatamaceous earth, along with some perlite and hay or the like).

author: @davereynolds6912
text:
The rocket stove is a neat way to optimize the burning of a fuel. Getting the most BTU's out of your fuel. As you have shown the mixing of hot air into the the exhaust stream encouraging secondary combustion is key. The two things I have found that helps is round shape on the exhaust encourages the gas to spin in the vortex shape and a double enclosure around the burn box using the gap inbetween to beat the air for the exhaust. 
Shape the hot air entrances into the exhaust stream so the spinning exhaust pulls the hot air into the stream.
Thank you for all your videos they keep my mind bouncing along😁😁

replies.author: @charlespatterson6037
replies.text:

Doesn’t this apply? If not, why not?
